title: When Charles the First Was King

memes.label: Battle of Edgehill
memes.description: The first major pitched battle of the English Civil War, fought on 23 October 1642 in Warwickshire. An indecisive engagement that demonstrated both sides were unprepared for the scale of the conflict.

memes.label: Battle of Marston Moor
memes.description: The largest battle of the English Civil War, fought on 2 July 1644 near York. A decisive Parliamentarian victory that ended Royalist power in northern England.

memes.label: Battle of Naseby
memes.description: The decisive battle of the First English Civil War, fought on 14 June 1645 in Northamptonshire. The New Model Army under Sir Thomas Fairfax and Oliver Cromwell destroyed the main Royalist field army.

memes.label: Battle of Powick Bridge
memes.description: The first significant cavalry engagement of the English Civil War, fought on 23 September 1642 near Worcester. A sharp Royalist victory that gave Prince Rupert his early reputation for invincibility.

memes.label: Ben Tuckett
memes.description: A loyal local ally of the Dale family, Ben Tuckett provides warnings and practical help to William Dale during the troubled times of the Civil War.

memes.label: Captain Trevor
memes.description: A Royalist cavalry officer in "When Charles the First Was King" who brings despatches to Yorkshire, becomes entangled in the lives of the Dale and Lisle families, and ultimately dies fighting at Newark alongside Philip Lisle.

memes.label: Cavalier
memes.description: A term for supporters of King Charles I during the English Civil War, associated with a distinctive style of dress, manners, and loyalty to the Crown.

memes.label: Charles I
memes.description: King of England, Scotland, and Ireland (1625–1649), whose conflict with Parliament led to the English Civil War and his execution at Whitehall. Central historical figure of the novel.

memes.label: Church of England
memes.description: The established Christian church of England, whose doctrine of obedience to the sovereign made most of its clergy natural Royalists during the English Civil War.

memes.label: Darrington
memes.description: A fictional Yorkshire village near Pontefract, home to the Dale family and the setting for much of the early and closing action in "When Charles the First Was King".

memes.label: Dennis Watson
memes.description: A member of the Watson family of Castle Hill, the long-standing rivals of the Dale family. His enmity with William Dale brings the local feud into the larger conflict of the Civil War.

memes.label: Earl of Strafford
memes.description: Thomas Wentworth, 1st Earl of Strafford, Charles I's most capable minister, whose execution by Parliament in 1641 cast a long shadow over the events leading to the English Civil War.

memes.label: English Civil War
memes.description: The armed conflict (1642–1651) between Royalists and Parliamentarians that forms the historical backdrop of the novel. Known to contemporaries as the Great Rebellion.

memes.label: Execution of Charles I
memes.description: The execution of King Charles I on 30 January 1649 at Whitehall, an unprecedented event that shocked Europe and established the principle that a monarch could be held accountable to law.

memes.label: House of Commons
memes.description: The elected lower house of the English Parliament, which became the centre of resistance to Charles I's royal prerogative and the driving force behind the Parliamentary cause in the Civil War.

memes.label: House of Lords
memes.description: The upper house of the English Parliament, composed of the peers of the realm and the bishops. During the Civil War, the Lords was divided between Royalist peers who joined the King and those who remained at Westminster.

memes.label: Ironsides
memes.description: The elite cavalry regiment of the Parliamentarian New Model Army, raised and trained by Oliver Cromwell. Known for their discipline, religious zeal, and decisive role in Parliamentarian victories.

memes.label: Jack Drumbleforth
memes.description: Son of Parson Drumbleforth of Darrington, a sturdy and loyal young Royalist soldier who fights alongside Philip Lisle and William Dale in the English Civil War.

memes.label: John Hampden
memes.description: John Hampden (1595–1643), a leading Parliamentarian statesman whose resistance to Charles I's Ship Money tax made him a symbol of constitutional opposition to royal prerogative.

memes.label: Long Parliament
memes.description: The Parliament that sat from November 1640 until 1660, whose conflict with Charles I triggered the English Civil War and which ultimately tried and executed the King.

memes.label: Marquis of Newcastle
memes.description: William Cavendish, Marquis of Newcastle, the senior Royalist commander in Yorkshire during the English Civil War. A wealthy nobleman who raised and funded his own army for the King's cause.

memes.label: Master Goodfellow
memes.description: A verger at Westminster Abbey and a loyal Royalist, Master Goodfellow hosts the narrator William Dale during his stay in London in the winter of 1648–49, including on the day of Charles I's execution.

memes.label: Master Stapleton
memes.description: A Royalist gentleman of Wentbridge, briefly encountered in the narrative, who provides a glimpse of the wider network of King's supporters in Yorkshire.

memes.label: Oliver Cromwell
memes.description: The leading Parliamentarian general of the English Civil War and later Lord Protector of England. Creator of the Ironsides cavalry and a central figure in the defeat of Charles I.

memes.label: Parliamentarian
memes.description: Those who supported the authority of Parliament against King Charles I during the English Civil War, advocating for constitutional limits on monarchical power.

memes.label: Parliamentarian Army
memes.description: The military forces raised by the English Parliament during the Civil War, which evolved from local militias into the New Model Army — the first professional standing army in English history.

memes.label: Parson Drumbleforth
memes.description: The Anglican clergyman of Darrington, a thoughtful Royalist who balances passionate loyalty to the Crown with honest recognition of the King's faults. A moral voice in the novel.

memes.label: Philip Lisle
memes.description: A gallant Royalist gentleman and soldier, close friend of the narrator William Dale. Known as "Black Phil" for his dark hair and daring exploits, he embodies the Cavalier ideal in the novel.

memes.label: Pikeman
memes.description: An infantry soldier armed with a pike — a long wooden spear typically 16–18 feet in length. Pikemen formed the defensive core of 17th-century infantry formations against cavalry charges.

memes.label: Pontefract Castle
memes.description: A formidable medieval fortress in Yorkshire and the last Royalist stronghold to surrender during the First English Civil War. The siege and defence of Pontefract Castle form the military centrepiece of the novel's second half.

memes.label: Presbyterian
memes.description: A Protestant religious and political movement during the English Civil War, advocating church governance by presbyteries (councils of elders) rather than bishops. A dominant force in the Long Parliament.

memes.label: Prince Rupert of the Rhine
memes.description: Nephew of Charles I and the most dynamic Royalist cavalry commander of the English Civil War. Known for his daring charges and the Royalist cavalry victories at Powick Bridge and Edgehill.

memes.label: Ride with the Despatches
memes.description: A pivotal plot event in "When Charles the First Was King" in which the narrator William Dale rides through the night to deliver Captain Trevor's despatches to the Marquis of Newcastle at York.

memes.label: Rose Lisle
memes.description: Sister of Philip Lisle, a gentle and spirited young woman of the West Riding who is the object of the narrator William Dale's silent affection.

memes.label: Roundhead
memes.description: Supporters of Parliament during the English Civil War, opponents of King Charles I and the Royalist cause. Also known as Parliamentarians.

memes.label: Royalist
memes.description: Supporters of King Charles I during the English Civil War, committed to the principle of monarchical authority and the divine right of kings.

memes.label: Ship Money
memes.description: A tax traditionally levied on coastal towns for naval defence, extended inland by Charles I without Parliamentary consent. One of the principal grievances leading to the English Civil War.

memes.label: Siege of Pontefract
memes.description: The Royalist siege and occupation of Pontefract Castle, a strategic Yorkshire stronghold where "kings have been imprisoned and done to death." A key local episode of the Civil War that directly involves the novel's narrator.

memes.label: Star Chamber
memes.description: A prerogative court under King Charles I, notorious for arbitrary rulings and used as a symbol of royal overreach by Parliamentarians. Abolished by the Long Parliament in 1641.

memes.label: Thomas Fairfax
memes.description: Sir Thomas Fairfax, 3rd Lord Fairfax of Cameron, the commander-in-chief of the Parliamentarian New Model Army. A Yorkshireman by birth, he led the forces that defeated the Royalists at Marston Moor and Naseby.

memes.label: Treason
memes.description: The legal and political concept of treason in 17th-century England — a charge that Parliament used against Charles I's ministers and ultimately against the King himself, transforming the English Civil War from rebellion into a constitutional crisis.

memes.label: Went Vale
memes.description: A picturesque valley near Darrington in Yorkshire, Went Vale is the setting for the narrator's first childhood meeting with Rose and Philip Lisle — a pastoral landscape that frames the novel's love story.

memes.label: William Dale
memes.description: The first-person narrator of the novel, a yeoman farmer of Dale's Field near Darrington, Yorkshire. Born in 1621, he recounts his experiences during the English Civil War as a reluctant participant in the Royalist cause.

memes.label: Yeoman
memes.description: A class of small landowning farmers in 17th-century England, ranking below the gentry but above cottagers and labourers. The narrator William Dale identifies himself as a yeoman.
